However, the closest relevant component is the `GeminiMultimedia` class in `extras/gemini_multimedia.py`, which provides comprehensive support for image (and other media) processing using the Gemini API. This class includes methods for processing images and could serve as the backend for an image search or analysis mini project.

Additionally, there are no scripts or notebooks in the `notebooks` or `examples` directories that are specifically dedicated to image search. The only related functionality is in `tests/tests_multimodal_input.py`, which demonstrates multimodal (including image) processing using the `GeminiMultimedia` class.

**Summary:**
- There is no standalone "search images mini project" file.
- The most relevant file for image processing/search is:  
  - `extras/gemini_multimedia.py` (core logic for image/media processing)
  - `tests/tests_multimodal_input.py` (example/test of multimodal input, including images)

If you are looking for a demo or script to run image search or analysis, `tests/tests_multimodal_input.py` is the closest example. If you need to build or locate a specific "search images" mini project, you may need to create a new script using the `GeminiMultimedia` class as the foundation.

We focus on standard web search engines here, but you can also read about the new world of generative AI search.<br/><br/>## Our Top Tested Picks<br/><br/> Best for Curated Interest Content<br/><br/>###  AOL Search <br/><br/>Jump To Details <br/><br/>$0.00 at Aol Search <br/><br/> See It <br/><br/> Best for Privacy, Customization, and AI Summaries<br/><br/>###  Brave Search <br/><br/>Jump To Details <br/><br/>$0.00 at Brave <br/><br/> See It <br/><br/> Best for Anonymity<br/><br/>###  DuckDuckGo <br/><br/>Jump To Details <br/><br/>$0.00 at DuckDuckGo <br/><br/> See It <br/><br/> Best for Supporting the Environment<br/><br/>###  Ecosia <br/><br/>Jump To Details <br/><br/>$0.00 at Ecosia <br/><br/> See It <br/><br/> Best for Emotion-Ranked Results<br/><br/>###  Mojeek <br/><br/>Jump To Details <br/><br/>$0.00 at Mojeek <br/><br/> See It <br/><br/> Best for Rich Results and AI Tools<br/><br/>###  Microsoft Bing <br/><br/>Jump To Details <br/><br/>$0.00 at Microsoft <br/><br/> See It <br/><br/> Best for Web3 Fans<br/><br/>###  Presearch <br/><br/>Jump To Details <br/><br/>$0.00 at Presearch.org <br/><br/> See It <br/><br/> Best Europe-Centric Private Search<br/><br/>###  Qwant <br/><br/>Jump To Details <br/><br/>$0.00 at Qwant <br/><br/> See It <br/><br/> Best for Music Search<br/><br/>###  Swisscows <br/><br/>Jump To Details <br/><br/>$0.00 at Swisscows <br/><br/> See It <br/><br/> Best for Anonymous Proxied Results<br/><br/>###  Startpage <br/><br/>Jump To Details <br/><br/>$0.00 at Startpage <br/><br/> See It <br/><br/> Best for News and Financial Information<br/><br/>###  Yahoo! <br/><br/>Jump To Details <br/><br/>$0.00 at Yahoo <br/><br/> See It <br/><br/> Best for Contributing to Content Creators<br/><br/>###  Yep <br/><br/>Jump To Details <br/><br/>$0.00 at Yep <br/><br/> See It <br/><br/> Best for Tuning Your Results<br/><br/>###  Kagi <br/><br/>Jump To Details <br/><br/>Starting at $5 Per Month at Kagi <br/><br/> See It <br/><br/> See (3) More <br/><br/> Deeper Dive: Our Top Tested Picks<br/><br/>* RELATED:<br/>* Best Web Browsers<br/>* Best Alternative Web Browsers<br/>* Best Private Web Browsers<br/><br/>## Best for Curated Interest Content<br/><br/>###  AOL Search <br/><br/>AOL is still in the web portal game, giving you a start page that shows curated hot topics in the entertainment, finance, food, health, news, and sports categories. But you can use it just as a web search engine as well. This offering features a slick interface, optional content filtering of potentially offensive material, and good image search filters. It's not the most private search in the world since activity tracking and location are on by default, but you can disable those from a Privacy Dashboard as long as you're signed in with an account. Yahoo now owns AOL, so your web results will likely be the same in both; they just have different interface tools.<br/><br/>GET IT NOW<br/><br/> See It <br/><br/>## Best for Privacy, Customization, and AI Summaries<br/><br/>###  Brave Search <br/><br/>Coming from the innovative creators of the privacy-focused Brave web browser, Brave Search claims “unmatched privacy.” In my testing using the EFF’s Cover Your Tracks Tools, Brave was the most private browser, so the claim has some weight behind it. When I enter a search in Brave Search, my ad-and-tracker blocker, uBlock, doesn’t report anything to block. Brave gets its results both from its web crawling index and “anonymous API calls to third parties.” Brave now gives you a choice of AI-powered or standard web results via its Answer With AI button. A Discussions section on the result page features content from Reddit and the like. Finally, Brave's Goggles feature lets you filter your results with parameters like No Celebrity content or News from the Left or Right. <br/><br/>GET IT NOW<br/><br/> See It <br/><br/>## Best for Anonymity<br/><br/>###  DuckDuckGo <br/><br/>The biggest name in private search deserves your attention. DuckDuckGo (DDG) has a simple privacy policy: “We don’t collect or share any of your personal information.” The search interface is simple, too, and results are on target, though they lack the extensive info panels found in Bing and Google. DDG lets you search for images, videos, news, maps, and shopping results. A nice touch is that DDG adds more header options depending on the search term, such as Definition, Meanings, Nutrition, and Recipes.<br/><br/>The search service now includes two AI features: Assist, for a generated answer summary for your query, and Duck.ai Chat. The first is similar to AI features in Bing, Brave, and Google. It summarizes web results to give you an answer at the top of the page so that you don't have to hunt down that nugget of information on multiple websites. Duck.ai lets you have a conversation with a chatbot that doesn't use your input for training. <br/><br/>My tracker blocker uBlock reported trackers on DuckDuckGo result pages, though they were from DDG itself. For the ultimate in privacy, DDG offers a Tor.onion version of its search engine. If you like this search engine, check out its private browser, too!<br/><br/>GET IT NOW<br/><br/> See It <br/><br/>## Best for Supporting the Environment<br/><br/>###  Ecosia <br/><br/>Ecosia makes people feel good about their web searches because it contributes to planting trees in environmentally sensitive areas. Ecosia's program has planted more than 230 million trees to date in collaboration with local environmental organizations around the world. This search site has improved its results pages, I can find what I need nearly all the time. <br/><br/>In addition to its altruistic endeavors, Ecosia is staying in the search game by adding AI tools. The best guess result at the top can often answer your quest with no further research required. There's now a Chat option, too, which is reminiscent of ChatGPT, writing things like cover letters for you in addition to finding info.<br/><br/>In terms of privacy, the site says it doesn't "create personal profiles of you based on your search history" and anonymizes all searches within one week. When I used the site with uBlock Origin active, a message asked me to disable it so the site could raise money. The site is up front about using cookies, which you can disable, but those tree bills don’t pay themselves. You can follow the organization's environmental efforts on YouTube and other social media sites.<br/><br/>GET IT NOW<br/><br/> See It <br/><br/>## Best for Emotion-Ranked Results<br/><br/>###  Mojeek <br/><br/>Mojeek is a UK-based search engine with a focus on privacy. Its index is completely built in-house, though it's considerably smaller than Bing's or Google's. The results are occasionally in an unexpected order, but you can usually find what you want. One clever feature is Mojeek's search by emotions, which uses deep machine learning to analyze the feels of a webpage. You enter your search term and then choose whether you want a result that’s happy, angry, sad, and so on. It also lets you search for images courtesy of Bing and Pixabay. A couple more differentiators are its custom Focus searches (for example, recipe search) and its Substack article search. <br/><br/>Mojeek doesn't have a lot in the way of prepackaged, graphically designed answers like you get with other search sites. For example, searching for Major League Baseball scores still just shows links. Its "Summarize these results" option uses an open-source LLM (Mixtral). Some result pages show a Wikipedia card, matching what a lot of higher-profile search sites do. In terms of Mojeek's security and privacy, uBlock didn't find any trackers it needed to block, and the site claims to be the first with a privacy policy stating that it doesn’t track you or sell your data.<br/><br/>GET IT NOW<br/><br/> See It <br/><br/>## Best for Rich Results and AI Tools<br/><br/>###  Microsoft Bing <br/><br/>For a search experience that's as close to Google in terms of richness of information on the result page—and, in many cases, even more useful instant result cards—Bing is it. Yes, it too is from a giant tech company, though one that’s a far cry from being a search monopoly. According to Statcounter, Bing receives less than 4% of search traffic worldwide, though that jumps to nearly 17% if you're just looking at US desktop search. Bing incorporates the Copilot AI search bot powered by ChatGPT. Separate from that chatbot is Copilot Search, which you can access after you see results. This is pretty cool, as it shows you its reasoning process and delivers curated results. Copilot is also good at providing links to its sources. Bing is the fullest-featured contender in this group, with strong news, image, video, and map search capabilities. It also offers voice search and can answer your queries out loud.<br/><br/>GET IT NOW<br/><br/> See It <br/><br/>## Best for Web3 Fans<br/><br/>###  Presearch <br/><br/>Privacy is the real mission of this decentralized Web3 search site. Presearch is the only service listed here that doesn’t run on a single company’s servers. Instead, it anonymizes your queries with distributed nodes, so a single provider doesn't save all your information. My uBlock tracking cookie blocker found nothing to block with Presearch.<br/><br/>If you sign in to an account, you can customize the search provider buttons from an extensive list, including Bing, DuckDuckGo, and Google. Signing in also lets you earn Presearch’s Ethereum-based crypto token, PRE, though only if you stick with Presearch's in-house search engine; there are caps on how much cryptocurrency you can earn each day. Anyone can run a node server in the distributed system to earn PRE rewards, and advertisers use PRE when bidding on keywords. Be aware that a lot of user reviews online claim the inability to withdraw their PRE tokens, however, so go for the decentralization and multi-search; don't expect a financial reward. I've had better luck with Bing Rewards on that front.<br/><br/>GET IT NOW<br/><br/> See It <br/><br/>## Best Europe-Centric Private Search<br/><br/>###  Qwant <br/><br/>From the EU comes the anti-Google search service Qwant. Qwant has an appealing design and uses first-party indexing robots to scan webpages for results, though it currently also uses APIs from Bing, iTunes, Twitter, and YouTube. Maps are based on the open-source OpenMapTiles, and you can privately save your map history. Qwant also makes a kid-friendly version of its search site called Qwant Junior. My search results using the engine were up to snuff.<br/><br/>Like many other options here, Qwant is getting into the AI act with its Qwant Next tool, but that's still in beta and only for users logged in to accounts. (I could create an account only after turning off my uBlock filtering, but I still wasn't able to access the advertised AI search.) As of 2025, Qwant has joined forces with Ecosia (see above) to build a Europe-centric search index to move away from reliance on anonymized Bing and other search index sources. Stay tuned for progress on this new direction.<br/><br/>Although uBlock showed several tracker cookies it deemed worthy of blocking, they were all from the qwant.com domain, which appears in one of uBlock’s block lists. On first use, a dialog box asks if you want the site to use cookies; one choice is to refuse all, which would solve that issue. The Qwant site clearly states, “We do not track or collect our users' data…When using Qwant, users can be certain that their browsing history isn’t stored, they aren’t profiled, and that they aren’t targeted through personalized ads.” <br/><br/>GET IT NOW<br/><br/> See It <br/><br/>## Best for Music Search<br/><br/>###  Swisscows <br/><br/>Switzerland-based Swisscows offers a VPN, a private messaging app, and private email, as well as this privacy-focused search site that donates to worthy causes when you use it. Nefarious sites and adult content are not part of Swisscows’ results, so it’s a good choice for younger users. In addition to having the typical Web, Images, and Video search result filters across the top, Swisscows has a Music option, which lets you play tunes directly on result pages courtesy of the free SoundCloud service. On the result page, you can use a clever Anonymous preview tool so that a site you want to visit doesn’t know you’re viewing it. (It shows an image of the site that’s not clickable, but you can read the whole page without any tracking.) The uBlock Origin extension didn’t have to block any suspicious trackers, a good sign for privacy.<br/><br/>GET IT NOW<br/><br/> See It <br/><br/>## Best for Anonymous Proxied Results<br/><br/>###  Startpage <br/><br/>Startpage uses privatized Google search results. The company makes a bold claim, saying it's the world’s most private search engine. You get the choice of Web, Images, Videos, and News categories for searches. Its Anonymous View option (accessible via a mask icon next to a result) lets you view a web page via a proxy server without revealing any of your information. The uBlock Origin extension blocks only Startpage's own ads and finds no third-party trackers. Startpage now offers a mobile app with one-click history wiping and the same proxy option as the web version. <br/><br/>GET IT NOW<br/><br/> See It <br/><br/>## Best for News and Financial Information<br/><br/>###  Yahoo! <br/><br/>The once-dominant and later-derided Yahoo Search has gotten its act together lately. The main Yahoo page has a search box at the top and panels for news, weather, sports, and trending searches, as well as a button for the company’s storied email service. If you head to search.yahoo.com, you now see a simpler page with trending searches and buttons for Yahoo's other services, including the highly regarded Yahoo Finance.<br/><br/>Yahoo's search result pages feature quick answers, though not in quite as polished a format as Bing and Google. That said, among the services here, Yahoo Search has the fullest answer pages after those two big players. Yahoo uses Bing to power search results, though it also has a unique panel of Trending searches and other features. Not to be left out of the AI party, Yahoo search provides a Microsoft Copilot-powered AI Chat option that itself uses OpenAI's ChatGPT AI models. <br/><br/>GET IT NOW<br/><br/> See It <br/><br/>## Best for Contributing to Content Creators<br/><br/>###  Yep <br/><br/>Like Patreon and similar platforms, Yep lets you tip your favorite online content creators. The difference is that you can earn money for tips through searching. Yep devotes 90% of its search ad revenue to this purpose. Lest you think Yep comes out of the blue, it's a creation from the founder of the prominent Ahrefs SEO tool and, in fact, uses that service for its web-crawling data. The search engine doesn't store or share any personally identifiable private info. Finally, Yep also includes an AI chatbot, which amusingly shows you its "thought" processes. Although the company doesn't disclose what powers that, its results resemble those from Microsoft Copilot, which is based on ChatGPT. Yep isn't going to win any speed contests, however.<br/><br/>GET IT NOW<br/><br/> See It <br/><br/>## Best for Tuning Your Results<br/><br/>###  Kagi <br/><br/>Kagi requires you to log in and pay a subscription fee, but for some users, that might be worth it. Good for research, it offers outstanding control over your results, including letting you favor specified domains over others. For example, you could set it to prioritize results from pcmag.com, something I highly recommend! It's completely private and ad-free, so your search history isn't being logged by Google to profile you. You can further customize your results using shortcuts, or "bangs" such as @pc for results from our esteemed website. Advanced search settings let you specify where a term should appear on a web page and the region for the result. Final Thoughts<br/><br/>## **What Is A Web Scraper**<br/><br/>A web scraper (also known as a web crawler) is a tool or a piece of code that performs the process of extracting data from web pages on the Internet. Various web scrapers have played an important role in the big data boom and make it easy for people to scrape the needed data. <br/><br/>If you’re interested in web scraping, you can read about the 10 myths about web scraping to find more information about its legacy, usage, cases, challenges, etc.<br/><br/>By reading this article, you can learn the top 10 **open-source web scrapers** and the best easy-to-use web scraper without any coding skills.<br/><br/>## **Best No-Coding Web Scraper – FREE**<br/><br/>Before learning about the top open-source web scrapers, you can learn the best alternative of them which can scrape data without any coding skills. Open-source web scrapers allow users to code based on their source code or framework, but also they have their limits. Especially for non-coding users, it’s hard to do with the customization and takes a lot of time. So, alternatives to open-source web scraper are necessary, especially for non-coding users.<br/><br/>**Octoparse**, as an easy-to-use web scraping tool, can extract data from any website to Excel with auto-detecting or preset templates. You can finish the whole scraping process within a few clicks, even though you know nothing about coding. <br/><br/>Try the online preset email and social media data scraper below without downloading any software, and just enter the parameters it asked for after you’ve previewed the data sample.<br/><br/><https://www.octoparse.com/template/email-social-media-scraper><br/><br/>However, Octoaprse is also suitable for those who are knowledgeable about coding, it provides advanced functions to customize your data scraping needs. For example, you can use the cloud scraping function to monitor your competitors’ situation scheduling. It also supports IP proxies and rotation to avoid being blocked.<br/><br/>octoparse en<br/><br/>Octoparse: Easy Web Scraping for Anyone<br/><br/>Free DownloadSign Up<br/><br/>Turn website data into structured Excel, CSV, Google Sheets, and your database directly.<br/><br/>Scrape data easily with auto-detecting functions, no coding skills are required.<br/><br/>Preset scraping templates for hot websites to get data in clicks.<br/><br/>Never get blocked with IP proxies and advanced API.<br/><br/>Cloud service to schedule data scraping at any time you want.<br/><br/>If you’re looking for a data service for your project, Octoparse data service is a good choice. We work closely with you to understand your data requirements and make sure we deliver what you desire. <br/><br/>## **Top 10 Open-source Web Scrapers**<br/><br/>### **1\. Scrapy**<br/><br/>**Language: Python**<br/><br/>Scrapy is the most popular open-source web crawler and collaborative web scraping tool in Python. It helps to extract data efficiently from websites, processes them as you need, and stores them in your preferred format(JSON, XML, and CSV). It’s built on top of a twisted asynchronous networking framework that can accept requests and process them faster. With Scrapy, you’ll be able to handle large web scraping projects in an efficient and flexible way.<br/><br/>**Advantages:**<br/><br/>* Fast and powerful<br/>* Easy to use with detailed documentation<br/>* Ability to plug new functions without having to touch the core<br/>* A healthy community and abundant resources<br/>* Cloud environment to run the scrapers<br/><br/>### **2\. Heritrix**<br/><br/>**Language: JAVA**<br/><br/>Heritrix is a JAVA-based open-source scraper with high extensibility and is designed for web archiving. It highly respects the robot.txt exclusion directives and Meta robot tags and collects data at a measured, adaptive pace unlikely to disrupt normal website activities. It provides a web-based user interface accessible with a web browser for operator control and monitoring of crawls.<br/><br/>**Advantages:**<br/><br/>* Replaceable pluggable modules<br/>* Web-based interface<br/>* With respect to the robot.txt and Meta robot tags<br/>* Excellent extensibility<br/><br/>### **3\. Web-Harvest**<br/><br/>**Language: JAVA**<br/><br/>Web-Harvest is an open-source scraper written in Java. It can collect useful data from specified pages. In order to do that, it mainly leverages techniques and technologies such as XSLT, XQuery, and Regular Expressions to operate or filter content from HTML/XML-based websites. It could be easily supplemented by custom Java libraries to augment its extraction capabilities.<br/><br/>**Advantages:**<br/><br/>* Powerful text and XML manipulation processors for data handling and control flow<br/>* The variable context for storing and using variables<br/>* Real scripting languages supported, which can be easily integrated within scraper configurations<br/><br/>### **4\. MechanicalSoup**<br/><br/>**Language: Python**<br/><br/>MechanicalSoup is a Python library designed to simulate the human’s interaction with websites when using a browser. It was built around Python giants Requests (for HTTP sessions) and BeautifulSoup (for document navigation). It automatically stores and sends cookies, follows redirects, follows links, and submits forms. If you try to simulate human behaviors like waiting for a certain event or clicking certain items rather than just scraping data, MechanicalSoup is really useful.<br/><br/>**Advantages:**<br/><br/>* Ability to simulate human behavior<br/>* Blazing fast for scraping fairly simple websites<br/>* Support CSS & XPath selectors<br/><br/>### **5\. Apify SDK**<br/><br/>**Language: JavaScript**<br/><br/>Apify SDK is one of the best web scrapers built in JavaScript. The scalable scraping library enables the development of data extraction and web automation jobs with headless Chrome and Puppeteer. With its unique powerful tools like RequestQueue and AutoscaledPool, you can start with several URLs and recursively follow links to other pages and can run the scraping tasks at the maximum capacity of the system respectively.<br/><br/>**Advantages:**<br/><br/>* Scrape with large and high-performance<br/>* Apify Cloud with a pool of proxies to avoid detection<br/>* Built-in support of Node.js plugins like Cheerio and Puppeteer<br/><br/>### **6\. Apache Nutch**<br/><br/>**Language: JAVA**<br/><br/>Apache Nutch, another open-source scraper coded entirely in Java, has a highly modular architecture, allowing developers to create plug-ins for media-type parsing, data retrieval, querying, and clustering. Being pluggable and modular, Nutch also provides extensible interfaces for custom implementations.<br/><br/>**Advantages:**<br/><br/>* Highly extensible and scalable<br/>* Obey txt rules<br/>* Vibrant community and active development<br/>* Pluggable parsing, protocols, storage, and indexing<br/><br/>### **7\. Jaunt**<br/><br/>**Language: JAVA**<br/><br/>Jaunt, based on JAVA, is designed for web scraping, web automation, and JSON querying. It offers a fast, ultra-light, and headless browser that provides web-scraping functionality, access to the DOM, and control over each HTTP Request/Response, but does not support JavaScript.<br/><br/>**Advantages:**<br/><br/>* Process individual HTTP Requests/Responses<br/>* Easy interfacing with REST APIs<br/>* Support for HTTP, HTTPS & basic auth<br/>* RegEx-enabled querying in DOM & JSON<br/><br/>### **8\. Node-crawler**<br/><br/>**Language: JavaScript**<br/><br/>Node-crawler is a powerful, popular, and production web crawler based on Node.js. It is completely written in Node.js and natively supports non-blocking asynchronous I/O, which provides great convenience for the crawler’s pipeline operation mechanism. At the same time, it supports the rapid selection of DOM, (no need to write regular expressions), and improves the efficiency of crawler development.<br/><br/>**Advantages:**<br/><br/>* Rate control<br/>* Different priorities for URL requests<br/>* Configurable pool size and retries<br/>* Server-side DOM & automatic jQuery insertion with Cheerio (default) or JSDOM<br/><br/>### **9\. PySpider**<br/><br/>**Language: Python**<br/><br/>PySpider is a powerful web crawler system in Python. It has an easy-to-use Web UI and a distributed architecture with components like a scheduler, fetcher, and processor. It supports various databases, such as MongoDB and MySQL, for data storage.<br/><br/>**Advantages:**<br/><br/>* Powerful WebUI with a script editor, task monitor, project manager, and result viewer<br/>* RabbitMQ, Beanstalk, Redis, and Kombu as the message queue<br/>* Distributed architecture<br/><br/>### **10\. StormCrawler**<br/><br/>**Language: JAVA**<br/><br/>StormCrawler is a full-fledged open-source web crawler. It consists of a collection of reusable resources and components, written mostly in Java. It is used for building low-latency, scalable, and optimized web scraping solutions in Java and also is perfectly suited to serve streams of inputs where the URLs are sent over streams for crawling.<br/><br/>**Advantages:**<br/><br/>* Highly scalable and can be used for large-scale recursive crawls<br/>* Easy to extend with additional libraries<br/>* Great thread management which reduces the latency of the crawl<br/><br/>## **Final Thoughts**<br/><br/>After learning about the top 10 open-source web scraping tools and their best alternative to get all the data without any coding skills. You can find another top 10 list of free web scrapers that are also easy to use. Choose the one most suitable for you to start your data scraping journey. Here are some of the **best open source and free web search tools** available in 2024, covering both general web search engines and developer-focused search frameworks:

---

### **General-Purpose Open Source Web Search Engines**

1. **[Brave Search](https://search.brave.com/)**
   - *Open source index, privacy-focused, no tracking, customizable, and offers AI summaries.*
   - Free to use, with advanced filtering and "Goggles" for custom result tuning.

2. **[DuckDuckGo](https://duckduckgo.com/)**
   - *Privacy-first, no personal data collection, open source components, and offers image/video/news search.*
   - Free and widely used for anonymous searching.

3. **[Mojeek](https://www.mojeek.com/)**
   - *Independent, open source crawler and index, privacy-focused, and offers emotion-based search filtering.*
   - Free to use.

4. **[Presearch](https://presearch.com/)**
   - *Decentralized, open source, privacy-centric, and rewards users with crypto tokens.*
   - Free and community-driven.

5. **[Qwant](https://www.qwant.com/)**
   - *Europe-based, privacy-focused, open source index, and no tracking.*
   - Free to use.

---

### **Open Source Search Frameworks for Developers**

If you want to build your own search engine or add search to your app/site:

1. **[Apache Solr](https://solr.apache.org/)**
   - *Enterprise-grade, open source, built on Apache Lucene, supports full-text, vector, and geospatial search.*
   - Highly scalable and used by many large organizations.

2. **[Meilisearch](https://www.meilisearch.com/)**
   - *Modern, open source, lightning-fast, easy to integrate, supports full-text and semantic search, and multi-modal (images, video, audio) search.*
   - Great for app/site search and developer projects.

3. **[Typesense](https://typesense.org/)**
   - *Open source, typo-tolerant, instant search engine, easy to deploy and use.*
   - Good for product, documentation, or site search.

4. **[Argus-AI-Websearch (GitHub)](https://github.com/jschw/Argus-AI-Websearch)**
   - *Open source Python tool for AI-boosted web search and research, with summarization features.*

---

### **Open Source Web Scrapers (for Custom Search/Data Extraction)**

- **[Scrapy](https://scrapy.org/)** (Python)
- **[MechanicalSoup](https://mechanicalsoup.readthedocs.io/)** (Python)
- **[Apify SDK](https://sdk.apify.com/)** (JavaScript)
- **[PySpider](https://github.com/binux/pyspider)** (Python)
- **[StormCrawler](https://stormcrawler.net/)** (Java)

These are not search engines, but can be used to build your own custom search/data extraction pipelines.

---

**Summary Table:**

| Tool/Engine      | Type         | Open Source | Free | Privacy | Use Case                        |
|------------------|--------------|-------------|------|---------|----------------------------------|
| Brave Search     | Search Engine| Yes         | Yes  | High    | General web search               |
| DuckDuckGo       | Search Engine| Partial     | Yes  | High    | General web search               |
| Mojeek           | Search Engine| Yes         | Yes  | High    | General web search               |
| Presearch        | Search Engine| Yes         | Yes  | High    | Decentralized web search         |
| Qwant            | Search Engine| Yes         | Yes  | High    | Europe-centric web search        |
| Apache Solr      | Framework    | Yes         | Yes  | N/A     | Build your own search engine     |
| Meilisearch      | Framework    | Yes         | Yes  | N/A     | App/site search, multi-modal     |
| Typesense        | Framework    | Yes         | Yes  | N/A     | App/site search                  |
| Scrapy, etc.     | Scraper      | Yes         | Yes  | N/A     | Custom data extraction/search    |

---

**If you want a ready-to-use web search engine:**  
Try Brave Search, DuckDuckGo, Mojeek, Presearch, or Qwant.

**If you want to build your own search experience:**  
Use Apache Solr, Meilisearch, or Typesense.

**If you want to scrape and search custom data:**  
Use Scrapy, MechanicalSoup, or PySpider.

Let me know if you want more details or setup instructions for any of these!

do all of these need api keys?

---

_**Assistant**_

No, **not all of these open source/free web search tools require API keys**. Here’s a breakdown:

---

### **General-Purpose Web Search Engines (No API Key Needed for Web Use)**
- **Brave Search**:  
  - **Web search:** No API key needed for normal use in your browser.
  - **API access:** API is available, but requires a key and may have usage limits.
- **DuckDuckGo**:  
  - **Web search:** No API key needed for browser use.
  - **API:** There is a free Instant Answer API (for some features), no key required, but it’s limited.
- **Mojeek**:  
  - **Web search:** No API key needed for browser use.
  - **API:** API available, may require registration.
- **Presearch**:  
  - **Web search:** No API key needed for browser use.
  - **API:** API available for developers, requires a key.
- **Qwant**:  
  - **Web search:** No API key needed for browser use.
  - **API:** API available, may require a key.

**Summary:**  
> **For normal web searching in your browser, you do NOT need an API key for any of these.**  
> **For programmatic/API access, most require a key and may have usage limits.**

---

### **Open Source Search Frameworks (Self-Hosted, No API Key Needed)**
- **Apache Solr, Meilisearch, Typesense**:  
  - **Self-hosted:** You install and run these on your own server or locally.
  - **No API key required** by default for local/private use.
  - **If you expose them to the public internet, you should add authentication for security, but it’s not required out of the box.**

---

### **Open Source Web Scrapers**
- **Scrapy, MechanicalSoup, PySpider, etc.:**
  - **No API key required** to use these libraries/tools.
  - **Note:** If you scrape sites that require authentication or have their own APIs, you may need keys for those specific sites.

---

### **Summary Table**

| Tool/Engine      | Web Use (No Key) | API Key for API? | Self-Hosted (No Key) |
|------------------|------------------|------------------|----------------------|
| Brave Search     | Yes              | Yes (for API)    | N/A                  |
| DuckDuckGo       | Yes              | No (limited API) | N/A                  |
| Mojeek           | Yes              | Maybe            | N/A                  |
| Presearch        | Yes              | Yes (for API)    | N/A                  |
| Qwant            | Yes              | Maybe            | N/A                  |
| Apache Solr      | N/A              | N/A              | Yes                  |
| Meilisearch      | N/A              | N/A              | Yes                  |
| Typesense        | N/A              | N/A              | Yes                  |
| Scrapy, etc.     | N/A              | N/A              | Yes                  |

---

**In summary:**  
- **Web search in your browser:** No API key needed.
- **API/programmatic access:** Usually requires a key.
- **Self-hosted frameworks:** No key needed unless you add your own authentication.

Let me know if you want details for a specific tool or use case!

*Called tool*: context7-mcp - resolve-library-id: Resolves a package/product name to a Context7-compatible library ID and returns a list of matching libraries.

You MUST call this function before 'get-library-docs' to obtain a valid Context7-compatible library ID UNLESS the user explicitly provides a library ID in the format '/org/project' or '/org/project/version' in their query.

Selection Process:
1. Analyze the query to understand what library/package the user is looking for
2. Return the most relevant match based on:
- Name similarity to the query (exact matches prioritized)
- Description relevance to the query's intent
- Documentation coverage (prioritize libraries with higher Code Snippet counts)
- Trust score (consider libraries with scores of 7-10 more authoritative)

Response Format:
- Return the selected library ID in a clearly marked section
- Provide a brief explanation for why this library was chosen
- If multiple good matches exist, acknowledge this but proceed with the most relevant one
- If no good matches exist, clearly state this and suggest query refinements

For ambiguous queries, request clarification before proceeding with a best-guess match.

*Parameters*:
  * libraryName: apache solr

*Result*:
Available Libraries (top matches):

Each result includes:
- Library ID: Context7-compatible identifier (format: /org/project)
- Name: Library or package name
- Description: Short summary
- Code Snippets: Number of available code examples
- Trust Score: Authority indicator
- Versions: List of versions if available. Use one of those versions if and only if the user explicitly provides a version in their query.

For best results, select libraries based on name match, trust score, snippet coverage, and relevance to your use case.

Tool use: **mcp_context7-mcp_get-library-docs**

<details>
          <summary>MCP tool call</summary>
      
*Called tool*: context7-mcp - get-library-docs: Fetches up-to-date documentation for a library. You must call 'resolve-library-id' first to obtain the exact Context7-compatible library ID required to use this tool, UNLESS the user explicitly provides a library ID in the format '/org/project' or '/org/project/version' in their query.

*Parameters*:
  * context7CompatibleLibraryID: /apache/solr
  * tokens: 4000

*Result*:
TITLE: Indexing DenseVectorField using XML
DESCRIPTION: This XML snippet demonstrates how to index documents with DenseVectorField using XML format. Each document contains an id and multiple vector fields representing the vector's components.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/query-guide/pages/dense-vector-search.adoc#_snippet_3

LANGUAGE: xml
CODE:
```
<add>
<doc>
<field name="id">1</field>
<field name="vector">1.0</field>
<field name="vector">2.5</field>
<field name="vector">3.7</field>
<field name="vector">4.1</field>
</doc>
<doc>
<field name="id">2</field>
<field name="vector">1.5</field>
<field name="vector">5.5</field>
<field name="vector">6.7</field>
<field name="vector">65.1</field>
</doc>
</add>
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: OpenAI Embedding Model Configuration
DESCRIPTION: This JSON configuration defines an embedding model using OpenAI. It requires an API key and a model name. It also includes optional parameters for baseUrl, timeout, logging, and retries.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/query-guide/pages/text-to-vector.adoc#_snippet_3

LANGUAGE: json
CODE:
```
{
  "class": "dev.langchain4j.model.openai.OpenAiEmbeddingModel",
  "name": "<a-name-for-your-model>",
  "params": {
    "baseUrl": "https://api.openai.com/v1",
    "apiKey": "<your-openAI-api-key>",
    "modelName": "<a-openAI-vectorisation-model>",
    "timeout": 60,
    "logRequests": true,
    "logResponses": true,
    "maxRetries": 5
  }
}
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Field Type Definition with Japanese Tokenizer and Filters by Name
DESCRIPTION: Defines a field type 'text_ja' in Solr using the Japanese tokenizer and various filters for Japanese language processing. It configures the tokenizer with 'search' mode and a user dictionary, and includes filters for base form extraction, part-of-speech stopping, CJK width normalization, stop word removal, katakana stemming, and lowercasing.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/indexing-guide/pages/language-analysis.adoc#_snippet_75

LANGUAGE: xml
CODE:
```
<fieldType name="text_ja" positionIncrementGap="100" autoGeneratePhraseQueries="false">
  <analyzer>
    <!-- Uncomment if you need to handle iteration marks: -->
    <!-- <charFilter name="japaneseIterationMark" /> -->
    <tokenizer name="japanese" mode="search" userDictionary="lang/userdict_ja.txt"/>
    <filter name="japaneseBaseForm"/>
    <filter name="japanesePartOfSpeechStop" tags="lang/stoptags_ja.txt"/>
    <filter name="cjkWidth"/>
    <filter name="stop" ignoreCase="true" words="lang/stopwords_ja.txt"/>
    <filter name="japaneseKatakanaStem" minimumLength="4"/>
    <filter name="lowercase"/>
  </analyzer>
</fieldType>
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: MistralAI Embedding Model Configuration
DESCRIPTION: This JSON configuration defines an embedding model using MistralAI. It requires an API key and a model name. It also includes optional parameters for baseUrl, timeout, logging, and retries.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/query-guide/pages/text-to-vector.adoc#_snippet_2

LANGUAGE: json
CODE:
```
{
  "class": "dev.langchain4j.model.mistralai.MistralAiEmbeddingModel",
  "name": "<a-name-for-your-model>",
  "params": {
    "baseUrl": "https://api.mistral.ai/v1",
    "apiKey": "<your-mistralAI-api-key>",
    "modelName": "<a-mistralAI-vectorisation-model>",
    "timeout": 60,
    "logRequests": true,
    "logResponses": true,
    "maxRetries": 5
  }
}
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Defining Node-Level Cache in solr.xml
DESCRIPTION: This XML snippet demonstrates how to define a node-level cache named 'myNodeLevelUserCache' using the <caches> element in solr.xml. It specifies the cache class as 'solr.CaffeineCache' and sets the size and initialSize attributes.  Node-level caches are singletons accessible via CoreContainer.getCache(String cacheName).
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/configuration-guide/pages/configuring-solr-xml.adoc#_snippet_8

LANGUAGE: xml
CODE:
```
<solr>
  <caches>
    <cache name="myNodeLevelUserCache"
           class="solr.CaffeineCache"
           size="4096"
           initialSize="1024" />
  </caches>
</solr>
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Enabling DocValues on a String Field in Solr Schema
DESCRIPTION: This XML snippet demonstrates how to enable DocValues for a string field in a Solr schema. By setting `docValues="true"`, the field will be stored in a column-oriented format, which is more efficient for operations like sorting and faceting.  The field is also configured to not be indexed or stored in the traditional way.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/indexing-guide/pages/docvalues.adoc#_snippet_0

LANGUAGE: xml
CODE:
```
<field name="manu_exact" type="string" indexed="false" stored="false" docValues="true" />
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Simple Terms Facet with JSON - curl
DESCRIPTION: This example demonstrates a simple terms facet using the JSON Facet API to partition documents into buckets based on the `cat` field and returns the top 3 categories. It uses a curl command to query the Solr techproducts collection.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/query-guide/pages/json-facet-api.adoc#_snippet_0

LANGUAGE: bash
CODE:
```
curl http://localhost:8983/solr/techproducts/query -d '
{
  "query": "*:*",
  "facet": {
    "categories" : {
      "type": "terms",
      "field": "cat",
      "limit": 3
    }
  }
}'
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Define a right ranking model in Solr LTR
DESCRIPTION: This JSON defines a linear ranking model named 'rightModel' that uses features from the 'commonFeatureStore'. It specifies the weights for each feature used in the model to calculate the ranking score. The weights are different from 'leftModel'.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/query-guide/pages/learning-to-rank.adoc#_snippet_51

LANGUAGE: JSON
CODE:
```
{
  "store" : "commonFeatureStore",
  "name" : "rightModel",
  "class" : "org.apache.solr.ltr.model.LinearModel",
  "features" : [
    { "name" : "documentRecency" },
    { "name" : "isBook" },
    { "name" : "originalScore" }
  ],
  "params" : {
    "weights" : {
      "documentRecency" : 1.0,
      "isBook" : 0.1,
      "originalScore" : 0.5
    }
  }
}
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Adding multiple fields to Solr schema using a list
DESCRIPTION: This example demonstrates how to add multiple fields ('shelf' and 'location') to a Solr schema using a single API call. It groups the 'add-field' commands within a list in the JSON payload. This approach is more concise when adding several fields at once.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/indexing-guide/pages/schema-api.adoc#_snippet_26

LANGUAGE: bash
CODE:
```
curl -X POST -H 'Content-type:application/json' --data-binary '{
  "add-field":[
     { "name":"shelf",
       "type":"myNewTxtField",
       "stored":true },
     { "name":"location",
       "type":"myNewTxtField",
       "stored":true }]
}' http://localhost:8983/solr/techproducts/schema
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Indexing a Document with SolrInputDocument in SolrJ
DESCRIPTION: This snippet demonstrates how to index a document in Solr using SolrJ with SolrInputDocument. It creates a SolrClient, builds a SolrInputDocument, adds fields to it, and adds the document to Solr. The example targets the 'techproducts' collection.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/deployment-guide/pages/solrj.adoc#_snippet_9

LANGUAGE: Java
CODE:
```
SolrClient client = getHttpSolrClient("techproducts");
SolrInputDocument doc = new SolrInputDocument();
doc.addField("id", "123");
doc.addField("name", "Test Product");
client.add(doc);
client.commit();
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Defining initParams with path and defaults in Solr
DESCRIPTION: This XML snippet defines an `<initParams>` section in Solr's `solrconfig.xml`. It sets the default search field (`df`) to `_text_` for request handlers with paths `/update/**`, `/query`, `/select`, `/tvrh`, `/elevate`, and `/spell`. This allows for a centralized definition of default parameters that can be overridden in individual request handler configurations.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/configuration-guide/pages/initparams.adoc#_snippet_0

LANGUAGE: xml
CODE:
```
<initParams path="/update/**,/query,/select,/tvrh,/elevate,/spell">
  <lst name="defaults">
    <str name="df">_text_</str>
  </lst>
</initParams>
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Example OpenAI Embedding Model Configuration
DESCRIPTION: This JSON configuration defines an example embedding model using OpenAI. It includes the class name, model name, API key, and other parameters.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/query-guide/pages/text-to-vector.adoc#_snippet_7

LANGUAGE: json
CODE:
```
{
  "class": "dev.langchain4j.model.openai.OpenAiEmbeddingModel",
  "name": "openai-1",
  "params": {
    "baseUrl": "https://api.openai.com/v1",
    "apiKey": "apiKey-openAI",
    "modelName": "text-embedding-3-small",
    "timeout": 60,
    "logRequests": true,
    "logResponses": true,
    "maxRetries": 5
  }
}
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Generating a Self-Signed Certificate with Keytool
DESCRIPTION: This command generates a self-signed certificate and a key using the keytool utility. It creates a keystore file named `solr-ssl.keystore.p12` which will be used for both server and client authentication. The `-ext SAN` option specifies allowed DNS names and IP addresses for hostname verification.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/deployment-guide/pages/enabling-ssl.adoc#_snippet_0

LANGUAGE: terminal
CODE:
```
$ keytool -genkeypair -alias solr-ssl -keyalg RSA -keysize 2048 -keypass secret -storepass secret -validity 9999 -keystore solr-ssl.keystore.p12 -storetype PKCS12 -ext SAN=DNS:localhost,IP:192.168.1.3,IP:127.0.0.1 -dname "CN=localhost, OU=Organizational Unit, O=Organization, L=Location, ST=State, C=Country"
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Indexing Multiple JSON Documents (JSON Lines) in Solr V2 API (Single-Node)
DESCRIPTION: This example demonstrates how to index multiple JSON documents in JSON Lines format using the `update/json` endpoint in a single-node Solr setup. Each line in the data payload represents a separate document. The Content-type header is set to application/json.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/indexing-guide/pages/transforming-and-indexing-custom-json.adoc#_snippet_23

LANGUAGE: bash
CODE:
```
curl 'http://localhost:8983/api/collections/techproducts/update/json' -H 'Content-type:application/json' -d '
{ "first":"Steve", "last":"Jobs", "grade":1, "subject":"Social Science", "test":"term1", "marks":90}
{ "first":"Steve", "last":"Woz", "grade":1, "subject":"Political Science", "test":"term1", "marks":86}'
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Executing SQL Query with JDBC Driver in Solr
DESCRIPTION: This code snippet demonstrates how to establish a connection to Solr using the JDBC driver, execute a SQL query, and process the results. It requires the SolrJ library and specifies the ZooKeeper host, collection name, aggregation mode, and number of workers in the connection URL. The code also includes proper resource management by closing the ResultSet, Statement, and Connection in a finally block.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/query-guide/pages/sql-query.adoc#_snippet_14

LANGUAGE: java
CODE:
```
Connection con = null;
try {
    con = DriverManager.getConnection("jdbc:solr://" + zkHost + "?collection=collection1&aggregationMode=map_reduce&numWorkers=2");
    stmt = con.createStatement();
    rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT a_s, sum(a_f) as sum FROM collection1 GROUP BY a_s ORDER BY sum desc");

    while(rs.next()) {
        String a_s = rs.getString("a_s");
        double s = rs.getDouble("sum");
    }
} finally {
    rs.close();
    stmt.close();
    con.close();
}
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Indexing Multiple Documents in Solr
DESCRIPTION: This command indexes multiple JSON documents into the 'techproducts' collection using the Solr Update API. The documents are provided as a JSON array, each representing a product with its attributes.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/getting-started/pages/tutorial-five-minutes.adoc#_snippet_4

LANGUAGE: bash
CODE:
```
curl --request POST \
  --url 'http://localhost:8983/api/collections/techproducts/update' \
  --header 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  --data '  [
  {
    "id" : "978-0641723445",
    "cat" : ["book","hardcover"],
    "name" : "The Lightning Thief",
    "author" : "Rick Riordan",
    "series_t" : "Percy Jackson and the Olympians",
    "sequence_i" : 1,
    "genre_s" : "fantasy",
    "inStock" : true,
    "price" : 12.50,
    "pages_i" : 384
  }
,
  {
    "id" : "978-1423103349",
    "cat" : ["book","paperback"],
    "name" : "The Sea of Monsters",
    "author" : "Rick Riordan",
    "series_t" : "Percy Jackson and the Olympians",
    "sequence_i" : 2,
    "genre_s" : "fantasy",
    "inStock" : true,
    "price" : 6.49,
    "pages_i" : 304
  }
]'
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Upload Configset using V1 API with pipe
DESCRIPTION: This example demonstrates how to upload a configset to Solr using the V1 API with a single request using a Unix pipe. It creates a zip file of the configset and pipes it directly to curl, sending it to the Solr admin configs endpoint with the UPLOAD action.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/configuration-guide/pages/configsets-api.adoc#_snippet_4

LANGUAGE: bash
CODE:
```
$ (cd server/solr/configsets/sample_techproducts_configs/conf && zip -r - *) | curl -X POST --header "Content-Type:application/octet-stream" --data-binary @- "http://localhost:8983/solr/admin/configs?action=UPLOAD&name=myConfigSet"
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Posting JSON data to Solr
DESCRIPTION: This command posts JSON data to a Solr server. It specifies the Solr URL and the collection name where the data will be indexed. The `example/films/films.json` file is used as the input data.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/indexing-guide/pages/post-tool.adoc#_snippet_0

LANGUAGE: bash
CODE:
```
$ bin/solr post --solr-url http://localhost:8983 --name gettingstarted example/films/films.json
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Example Solr Query Response with Features
DESCRIPTION: This JSON shows an example response from a Solr query with feature logging enabled. The `[features]` field contains a comma-separated list of feature names and their corresponding values.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/query-guide/pages/learning-to-rank.adoc#_snippet_23

LANGUAGE: json
CODE:
```
{
  "responseHeader":{
    "status":0,
    "QTime":0,
    "params":{
      "q":"test",
      "fl":"id,score,[features]"}},
  "response":{"numFound":2,"start":0,"maxScore":1.959392,"docs":[
      {
        "id":"GB18030TEST",
        "score":1.959392,
        "[features]":"documentRecency=0.020893794,isBook=0.0,originalScore=1.959392"},
      {
        "id":"UTF8TEST",
        "score":1.5513437,
        "[features]":"documentRecency=0.020893794,isBook=0.0,originalScore=1.5513437"}]}
}
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Configuring JWT Authentication and External Role-Based Authorization in Solr
DESCRIPTION: This JSON snippet configures Solr to use JWT Authentication with the ExternalRoleRuleBasedAuthorizationPlugin. It specifies the JWKS URL, the claim containing user roles ('rolesClaim'), and grants the 'admin' role permission to edit security settings.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/deployment-guide/pages/rule-based-authorization-plugin.adoc#_snippet_3

LANGUAGE: json
CODE:
```
{
"authentication":{
   "class": "solr.JWTAuthPlugin",
   "jwksUrl": "https://my.key.server/jwk.json",
   "rolesClaim": "roles"
},
"authorization":{
   "class":"solr.ExternalRoleRuleBasedAuthorizationPlugin",
   "permissions":[{"name":"security-edit",
      "role":"admin"}]
}}
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Configuring Analyzer Chain with Name Attributes in Solr
DESCRIPTION: This snippet demonstrates how to configure an analyzer chain using the name attribute to specify tokenizer and filter factories.  It uses standard tokenizer, lowercase filter, stop filter, and english porter stemmer.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/indexing-guide/pages/analyzers.adoc#_snippet_1

LANGUAGE: xml
CODE:
```
<fieldType name="nametext" class="solr.TextField">
  <analyzer>
    <tokenizer name="standard"/>
    <filter name="lowercase"/>
    <filter name="stop"/>
    <filter name="englishPorter"/>
  </analyzer>
</fieldType>
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Configuring RealTimeGetHandler in solrconfig.xml
DESCRIPTION: This XML snippet shows how to configure the RealTimeGetHandler in Solr's solrconfig.xml file.  It maps the `/get` request handler to the `solr.RealTimeGetHandler` class. The `omitHeader` parameter is set to `true` to exclude the header from the response.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/configuration-guide/pages/realtime-get.adoc#_snippet_1

LANGUAGE: xml
CODE:
```
<requestHandler name="/get" class="solr.RealTimeGetHandler">
  <lst name="defaults">
    <str name="omitHeader">true</str>
  </lst>
</requestHandler>
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Define 'vectorisation' Update Request Processor Chain for Partial Updates
DESCRIPTION: Defines an update request processor chain named 'vectorisation' that includes only the TextToVectorUpdateProcessorFactory (configured with inputField, outputField, and model parameters) and the RunUpdateProcessorFactory. This chain is used for partial updates to add text vectors to existing documents.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/query-guide/pages/text-to-vector.adoc#_snippet_11

LANGUAGE: xml
CODE:
```
<updateRequestProcessorChain name="vectorisation">
<processor class="solr.llm.textvectorisation.update.processor.TextToVectorUpdateProcessorFactory">
   <str name="inputField">_text_</str>
   <str name="outputField">vector</str>
   <str name="model">dummy-1</str>
  </processor>
  <processor class="solr.RunUpdateProcessorFactory"/>
 </updateRequestProcessorChain>
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Requesting Results Export with /export Handler
DESCRIPTION: This example demonstrates how to make a request to the `/export` handler to export the result set of a query.  It includes the `q`, `sort`, and `fl` parameters, which are all required.  The `sort` parameter specifies the fields to sort by and the `fl` parameter specifies the fields to include in the result set.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/query-guide/pages/exporting-result-sets.adoc#_snippet_0

LANGUAGE: text
CODE:
```
http://localhost:8983/solr/core_name/export?q=my-query&sort=severity+desc,timestamp+desc&fl=severity,timestamp,msg
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Indexing XML Documents into Solr
DESCRIPTION: This command adds all XML documents starting with `article` and having the `.xml` extension to the `gettingstarted` collection on a Solr instance running on localhost port 8984. It uses the `bin/solr post` tool to send the documents to Solr for indexing.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/indexing-guide/pages/post-tool.adoc#_snippet_4

LANGUAGE: console
CODE:
```
$ bin/solr post --solr-url http://localhost:8984 --name gettingstarted article*.xml
```

DESCRIPTION: This JSON example demonstrates how to index deeply nested documents in Solr, representing a product with associated SKUs and manuals. The example showcases the structure of parent-child relationships using pseudo-fields like 'skus' and 'manuals' to define nested documents within a product document.  It includes a product with multiple SKUs, some of which have their own nested manuals.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/indexing-guide/pages/indexing-nested-documents.adoc#_snippet_0

LANGUAGE: json
CODE:
```
[{
  "id": "P11!prod",
  "name_s": "Swingline Stapler",
  "description_t": "The Cadillac of office staplers ...",
  "skus": [ { "id": "P11!S21",
              "color_s": "RED",
              "price_i": 42,
              "manuals": [ { "id": "P11!D41",
                             "name_s": "Red Swingline Brochure",
                             "pages_i":1,
                             "content_t": "..."
                           } ]
            },
            { "id": "P11!S31",
              "color_s": "BLACK",
              "price_i": 3
            } ],
  "manuals": [ { "id": "P11!D51",
                 "name_s": "Quick Reference Guide",
                 "pages_i":1,
                 "content_t": "How to use your stapler ..."
               },
               { "id": "P11!D61",
                 "name_s": "Warranty Details",
                 "pages_i":42

```

TITLE: Running Solr with environment variable
DESCRIPTION: This command demonstrates how to run a Solr Docker container with an environment variable to configure the heap size. It maps port 8983 and sets the `SOLR_HEAP` environment variable.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/deployment-guide/pages/solr-in-docker.adoc#_snippet_18

LANGUAGE: bash
CODE:
```
docker run -d -p 8983:8983 -e SOLR_HEAP=800m solr
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: KNN Text to Vector Query Example
DESCRIPTION: This example shows a knn_text_to_vector search that encodes the query 'hello world query' into a vector using the 'a-model' model and retrieves the 10 nearest documents based on the 'vector' field.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/query-guide/pages/dense-vector-search.adoc#_snippet_6

LANGUAGE: text
CODE:
```
?q={!knn_text_to_vector model=a-model f=vector topK=10}hello world query
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Cosine Similarity Function Example
DESCRIPTION: Demonstrates the use of the cosineSimilarity function with two array parameters. The function calculates the cosine similarity between the two arrays.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/query-guide/pages/vector-math.adoc#_snippet_18

LANGUAGE: text
CODE:
```
cosineSimilarity(array(2,3,0,0,0,1), array(2,0,1,0,0,3))
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Basic Nodes Function Syntax - Solr
DESCRIPTION: This is the most basic syntax for the `nodes` function. It traverses the `emails` collection, starting from the node `johndoe@apache.org` in the `from` field, and gathers the values in the `to` field. This returns all the edges in the index that have `johndoe@apache.org` in the `from` field.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/query-guide/pages/graph-traversal.adoc#_snippet_0

LANGUAGE: Solr
CODE:
```
nodes(emails,
      walk="johndoe@apache.org->from",
      gather="to")
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Logging Features for a Specific Feature Store via HTTP
DESCRIPTION: This HTTP endpoint logs features for the `currentFeatureStore` feature store during a query. The `fl` parameter specifies the fields to return, including the feature values.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/query-guide/pages/learning-to-rank.adoc#_snippet_10

LANGUAGE: text
CODE:
```
http://localhost:8983/solr/techproducts/query?q=test&fl=id,score,[features store=currentFeatureStore]
```

TITLE: Pattern Replace Filter with Name
DESCRIPTION: This XML configuration defines an analyzer with a standard tokenizer and a pattern replace filter. The filter replaces all occurrences of 'cat' with 'dog'.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/indexing-guide/pages/filters.adoc#_snippet_72

LANGUAGE: xml
CODE:
```
<analyzer>
  <tokenizer name="standard"/>
  <filter name="patternReplace" pattern="cat" replacement="dog"/>
</analyzer>
```

TITLE: Debugging Solr DisMax Query
DESCRIPTION: This example demonstrates how to use the 'debugQuery=true' parameter to get detailed information about how a Solr DisMax query is parsed and executed, including score explanations for each document.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/query-guide/pages/dismax-query-parser.adoc#_snippet_17

LANGUAGE: Solr
CODE:
```
\http://localhost:8983/solr/techproducts/select?defType=dismax&q=belkin+ipod+gibberish&debugQuery=true
```

LANGUAGE: Solr
CODE:
```
\http://localhost:8983/solr/techproducts/select?defType=dismax&q=video+card&debugQuery=true
```

TITLE: Update Request Handler via Config API (V1)
DESCRIPTION: This code snippet demonstrates how to update an existing request handler in Solr using the Config API. It sends a POST request with a JSON payload to the /config endpoint, modifying the handler's defaults and useParams.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/configuration-guide/pages/config-api.adoc#_snippet_20

LANGUAGE: bash
CODE:
```
curl -X POST -H 'Content-type:application/json' -d '{
  "update-requesthandler": {
    "name": "/mypath",
    "class": "solr.DumpRequestHandler",
    "defaults": {"x": "new value for X", "rows": "20"},
    "useParams": "x"
  }
}' http://localhost:8983/solr/techproducts/config
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Set updateHandler.autoCommit.maxTime Property (V2 API)
DESCRIPTION: This example shows how to use the Solr Config API V2 to set the `updateHandler.autoCommit.maxTime` property to 15000 milliseconds. It uses a `curl` command to send a POST request with a JSON payload to the Solr config endpoint.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/configuration-guide/pages/config-api.adoc#_snippet_9

LANGUAGE: Bash
CODE:
```
curl -X POST -H 'Content-type: application/json' -d '{"set-property":{"updateHandler.autoCommit.maxTime":15000}}' http://localhost:8983/api/collections/techproducts/config
```

TITLE: Reload Collection V2 API
DESCRIPTION: This example demonstrates reloading a Solr collection using the V2 API with an optional asynchronous request ID. The `async` parameter allows tracking the asynchronous operation.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/deployment-guide/pages/collection-management.adoc#_snippet_4

LANGUAGE: bash
CODE:
```
curl -X POST http://localhost:8983/api/collections/techproducts_v2/reload -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '
  {
    "async": "someAsyncId"
  }
'
```

TITLE: Add a New Package Version
DESCRIPTION: This bash command uses `curl` to add a new version of a package to Solr using the package management API. It specifies the package name, version, and the file path to the JAR file. The `Content-type` header is set to `application/json`, and the request body is a JSON object containing the package information.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/configuration-guide/pages/package-manager-internals.adoc#_snippet_22

LANGUAGE: bash
CODE:
```
curl  http://localhost:8983/api/cluster/package -H 'Content-type:application/json' -d  '{"add": {"package" : "mypkg", "version":"2.0", "files" :["/mypkg/2.0/myplugins.jar"]}}'
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Starting Solr in techproducts example
DESCRIPTION: This command starts a Solr instance using the 'techproducts' example configuration. This is a prerequisite for running the collection management commands shown in the following examples.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/deployment-guide/pages/collection-management.adoc#_snippet_0

LANGUAGE: bash
CODE:
```
bin/solr start -e techproducts
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Creating a Solr collection
DESCRIPTION: This example shows how to create a new Solr collection using the `bin/solr` script. Replace `<yourCollection>` with the desired name for the new collection. The command also specifies the number of shards and replication factor.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/getting-started/pages/tutorial-techproducts.adoc#_snippet_11

LANGUAGE: bash
CODE:
```
$ bin/solr create -c <yourCollection> --shards 2 -rf 2
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Configuring IP Access Control with Allowlist in Bash
DESCRIPTION: This snippet demonstrates how to configure IP access control in Solr using the SOLR_IP_ALLOWLIST environment variable. It allows access from IPv4/IPv6 localhost, the 192.168.0.x IPv4 network, and the 2000:123:4:5:: IPv6 network. This configuration restricts network access to specific hosts.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/deployment-guide/pages/securing-solr.adoc#_snippet_0

LANGUAGE: bash
CODE:
```
# Allow IPv4/IPv6 localhost, the 192.168.0.x IPv4 network, and 2000:123:4:5:: IPv6 network.
SOLR_IP_ALLOWLIST="127.0.0.1, [::1], 192.168.0.0/24, [2000:123:4:5::]/64"
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Index JSON Documents with Simplified Field Mapping (V2 API SolrCloud)
DESCRIPTION: This example demonstrates indexing JSON documents in SolrCloud using the V2 API, splitting the document at the 'exams' field and using simplified field mapping. The curl command sends a JSON payload to the Solr endpoint, specifying the split and field paths.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/indexing-guide/pages/transforming-and-indexing-custom-json.adoc#_snippet_6

LANGUAGE: bash
CODE:
```
curl 'http://localhost:8983/api/collections/techproducts/update/json/docs'\
'?split=/exams'\
'&f=/first'\
'&f=/last'\
'&f=/grade'\
'&f=/exams/subject'\
'&f=/exams/test'\
'&f=/exams/marks'\
 -H 'Content-type:application/json' -d '
{
  "first": "John",
  "last": "Doe",
  "grade": 8,
  "exams": [
    {
      "subject": "Maths",
      "test"   : "term1",
      "marks"  : 90},
    {
      "subject": "Biology",
      "test"   : "term1",
      "marks"  : 86}
  ]
}'
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Adding a Stopword to Solr
DESCRIPTION: This snippet demonstrates how to add a new word to the English stop word list in Solr using an HTTP PUT request. It uses curl to send a JSON list containing the word to be added to the specified endpoint.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/configuration-guide/pages/managed-resources.adoc#_snippet_5

LANGUAGE: bash
CODE:
```
curl -X PUT -H 'Content-type:application/json' --data-binary '["foo"]' "http://localhost:8983/solr/techproducts/schema/analysis/stopwords/english"
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Config API JSON example for overriding properties
DESCRIPTION: This JSON snippet shows an example of a configoverlay.json file, which is used to store configuration changes made via the Config API. It includes user-defined properties and request handler configurations.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/configuration-guide/pages/property-substitution.adoc#_snippet_2

LANGUAGE: json
CODE:
```
{
  "userProps":{"update.autoCreateFields":"false"},
  "requestHandler":{"/myterms":{
      "name":"/myterms",
      "class":"solr.SearchHandler",
      "defaults":{
        "terms":true,
        "distrib":false},
      "components":["terms"]}}}
}
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Collection Status V1 API Request
DESCRIPTION: This example shows how to use the COLSTATUS action in the V1 API to get detailed information about a collection's status, including core information, segment details, field information, and size information. It uses query parameters to specify the collection and the desired information.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/deployment-guide/pages/collection-management.adoc#_snippet_24

LANGUAGE: bash
CODE:
```
http://localhost:8983/solr/admin/collections?action=COLSTATUS&collection=techproducts_v2&coreInfo=true&segments=true&fieldInfo=true&sizeInfo=true
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Configuring Analyzer Chain with Class Attributes in Solr
DESCRIPTION: This snippet demonstrates how to configure an analyzer chain using the class attribute to specify tokenizer and filter factories. It uses standard tokenizer, lowercase filter, stop filter, and english porter stemmer. Note the solr. prefix shorthand.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/indexing-guide/pages/analyzers.adoc#_snippet_2

LANGUAGE: xml
CODE:
```
<fieldType name="nametext" class="solr.TextField">
  <analyzer>
    <tokenizer class="solr.StandardTokenizerFactory"/>
    <filter class="solr.LowerCaseFilterFactory"/>
    <filter class="solr.StopFilterFactory"/>
    <filter class="solr.EnglishPorterFilterFactory"/>
  </analyzer>
</fieldType>
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Configuring Update Log in solrconfig.xml
DESCRIPTION: This XML snippet shows how to configure the update log in Solr's solrconfig.xml file. The update log is essential for the Real Time Get feature to function correctly. The 'dir' attribute specifies the directory where the update log is stored.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/configuration-guide/pages/realtime-get.adoc#_snippet_0

LANGUAGE: xml
CODE:
```
<updateLog>
  <str name="dir">${solr.ulog.dir:}</str>
</updateLog>
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Add a New Field Type - V2 API with Two Analyzers
DESCRIPTION: Adds a new field type to the Solr schema using the V2 API. This example creates a field type named 'myNewTextField' with separate indexAnalyzer and queryAnalyzer sections. The indexAnalyzer uses the pathHierarchy tokenizer, and the queryAnalyzer uses the keyword tokenizer.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/indexing-guide/pages/schema-api.adoc#_snippet_15

LANGUAGE: bash
CODE:
```
curl -X PUT -H 'Content-type:application/json' --data-binary '{
   "class":"solr.TextField",
   "indexAnalyzer":{
      "tokenizer":{
         "name":"pathHierarchy",
         "delimiter":"/" }},
   "queryAnalyzer":{
      "tokenizer":{
         "name":"keyword" }}
}' http://localhost:8983/api/collections/techproducts/schema/fieldtypes/myNewTextField
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Custom script execution in Solr Docker container
DESCRIPTION: This example shows how to run a custom script inside the Solr Docker container before Solr starts. It mounts a shell script to the `/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/` directory.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/deployment-guide/pages/solr-in-docker.adoc#_snippet_19

LANGUAGE: bash
CODE:
```
#!/bin/bash
set -e
echo "this is running inside the container before Solr starts"
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Filter Cache Configuration with Size
DESCRIPTION: Configures the filter cache using CaffeineCache with specified size, initial size, and autowarm count. The filter cache stores parsed queries and matching documents, improving performance for subsequent queries with the same filter.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/configuration-guide/pages/caches-warming.adoc#_snippet_1

LANGUAGE: xml
CODE:
```
<filterCache class="solr.CaffeineCache"
             size="512"
             initialSize="512"
             autowarmCount="128"/>
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Calculate Market Basket Co-occurrence in Solr
DESCRIPTION: This example calculates market basket co-occurrence using a simple market basket table indexed in Solr. It finds the products that most frequently co-occur with product "ABC" in past shopping baskets by traversing a graph of basketIDs and productIDs.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/query-guide/pages/graph-traversal.adoc#_snippet_13

LANGUAGE: Solr Query Syntax
CODE:
```
top(n="5",
    sort="count(*) desc",
    nodes(baskets,
          random(baskets, q="productID:ABC", fl="basketID", rows="500"),
          walk="basketID->basketID",
          fq="-productID:ABC",
          gather="productID",
          count(*)))
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Register Request Handler
DESCRIPTION: Registers a request handler in Solr using the specified class. This snippet shows how to define a request handler in solrconfig.xml, specifying the class name and path.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/configuration-guide/pages/package-manager.adoc#_snippet_8

LANGUAGE: xml
CODE:
```
<requestHandler name="/myhandler" class="mypackage:full.path.to.MyClass"></requestHandler>
```

----------------------------------------

TITLE: Tagging Queries and Filters in JSON - SolrJ
DESCRIPTION: This example demonstrates how to tag queries and filters using the JSON Query DSL in Solr with SolrJ. It creates two tagged clauses: `titleTag` and `inStockTag`. The tags are used to identify specific parts of the query for later reference or exclusion.
SOURCE: https://github.com/apache/solr/blob/main/solr/solr-ref-guide/modules/query-guide/pages/json-query-dsl.adoc#_snippet_17

LANGUAGE: java
CODE:
```
include::example$JsonRequestApiTest.java[tag=solrj-tagged-query]
```
</details>
